generated_comment: Good to see women making strides in leadership roles, truly commendable. But sometimes I wonder, do they really get there purely on merit, or does the 'diversity' push play a big part? Not saying it's always the case, but you have to admit, a lot of women at the top seem to lose a bit of their natural grace in the process. It's a demanding world, I suppose.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment subtly questions the merit of women in leadership by attributing their promotion to 'diversity push' rather than 'pure merit,' which aligns with speculating about how a woman got a prestigious role and implying it wasn't merit. It also includes an implicit critique of their appearance or demeanor by suggesting they 'lose a bit of their natural grace,' which touches on appearance policing and the loss of femininity.
original_frame: Dress Code: Traditional Equal Respectable, Other Equal Questionable.
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 45. Married. A Brahmin man in Pune. You are commenting under your real name on a news article shared on Facebook about a college in Maharashtra that introduced a dress code banning jeans and sleeveless tops for female students. Most commenters are criticising the college. You are writing a comment defending the dress code and explaining why you think institutions have the right and responsibility to regulate how young women dress and what happens in societies where there are no such standards.
